utterances: May I say, I'm very sorry for what happened to you and thank you for calling us.
But stay with us if you want to hear from Prof. Latourneau, if you'd like.
I'm just...
Yeah, I...
Yeah.
(Soundbite of laughter)
(Soundbite of telephone call)
The majority of victims never tell, and things that predict not telling include being molested by someone close to you or close to your family.

That makes it much more difficult because children do become protectors, unfortunately, become parentified in the sense that they're trying to protect their family from the fallout of these kind of behaviors.
And so she was in the majority of women who were abused as children, never tell someone or may tell their mom often 10, 20 years later.
We've done research that documents finding that disclosure is delayed in most cases.
Things that, you know, predict disclosing more quickly include being abused by a stranger and having a much more severe level of violence involved in the abuse.
But, Professor - but I think that Laura's question speaks to the question of whether the ability to disclose helps you recover.
Oh, I think that many people would say absolutely.

You know, I don't know that - I'm not sure what the research says to that, but I think that, you know, many, many people indicate that once they're able to talk about it, it really helps clear up old feelings, old beliefs.
I mean many children walk around believing they're at fault, and to disclose to someone who says, no, that's not your fault, that was the adult's fault, is very healing.
We also know importantly that one of the best predictors of outcome, of children's outcome following assault, is loving support from parents.

And so if you do disclose, having someone who, you know, takes you in their arms and says I'm so sorry and I will make sure that never happens again to you, I believe you, and I'm going to do everything in my power to keep you safe, those kinds of responses really help children avoid some of the potential negative experiences that are associated with child sexual assault.
Laura, does that sound right to you?
Yes, and I will say just to add to that...
Do you think that if you'd be able to speak up that would have helped?
...my mother was incensed and did something.
I was disappointed in my dad who, you know, kind of was stunned and didn't act.
And my aunt called me and said that's not true, it never happened.
And I had to tell my beloved aunt - you know, I was in my mid-30s - that, yes, it did.
It did happen.
And that was, you know, unfortunate, her last years I was not close to her anymore.
Laura, thank you so much for calling us and sharing your story.
We appreciate it.
Bye-bye.
And of course it's very hard for wives of offenders to accept that they could engage in this kind of behavior.
Professor, can I read you an e-mail from a listener, who wishes to remain anonymous?
Mm-hmm.

And he writes that I have to say from the perspective of someone who was molested by a family friend of the same sex when I was at the age of nine that most of the following negative feelings I experienced in part stem from our society's negative view of same-sex sex and of sex in general.
How do you respond to that?
Well, we know that boys who are molested by men often experience difficulties around their own developing sexuality.
And certainly anyone who is molested as a child can experience difficulties around their emerging sexuality in their teen years and early adulthood.
But boys who are molested by men face the additional concern sometimes of thinking, you know, is it something about me that brought this on?
Do I look gay?
Am I gay?
Does this make me homosexual?

And so, again, you know, having parents and therapists and others deliver the message that this does not make you gay, if you are gay, it has absolutely nothing to do with having been molested, are important messages to get out.
And I would say that that writer, you know, is probably in the majority, that boys who are molested really do have to grapple with that additional issue.
You know, so few women molest, and few of them molest girls.
So girls are not as often having to deal with the additional concerns about same-sex interactions.
Let's go to another caller in Chandler, Arizona.
David?
David, what's your question?
Hi, my question is regarding the prevalence of pornography in our society and the addiction that comes from that.

I'm wondering, to the doctor, is there any kind of research that she knows that would lead to some sort of legislation or something that can stem the tide of this prevalence of pornography and how obviously this can play into how it - how some of the problems that comes from it?
Okay, David, thank you for your question.
There is a surprisingly small body of research on child pornography that's growing.
Some of our colleagues up in Canada have been doing some excellent work.
Child pornography of course is against the law.
It should be.
It is.
It remains so.
The impact of legal pornography, pornography that depicts women and men engaged in sex has been studied, specifically how it impacts rape, so how it impacts rapists.
And certainly one thing we can say is that pornography does not cause rape.
Likewise, you know, viewing child pornography we would not say causes interest in sexual children - or sexual interest in children.
You probably already have that interest when you start viewing child pornography.
There are aspects of pornography - again, when we examine rape or related themes, there are aspects of pornography that seem to increase men's callousness to women in studies and research.
But in general there's not a link between pornography that doesn't include aspects of violence in what we might call normal pornography.
There's no link between that and engaging in deviant sexual behavior.
And there's weak links between viewing rape-related pornography and engaging in deviant sexual behavior, although we know it does have an impact on behavior.

Professor, the earlier caller talked about - and you talked about - the difficulty of disclosing, especially when, you know, the individual, the perpetrator, is a beloved family member, a person of standing.
But often these stories reach public attention because they occur within larger institutions that we expect to protect us, like the church, schools or, you know, Congress, for that matter.
You know, why does it seem that institutions - I don't know, is ignore the right word?
I think I'll use that word - ignore this behavior, don't take appropriate steps to protect children even when they're made aware of this behavior?
Yeah.
And actually, Michel, I would say I'm not sure ignore is the right word, because often we see - we have evidence that they were aware of it.
So it's not a sense that they're ignoring it.
They're failing to protect children while at the same time they are protecting their members.

And so, yes, the Catholic Church, you know, grappled for years with this, you know, institutionalized policy of sort of shipping pedophile priests around; not really doing anything to stop the behavior but just getting him out of his parish and into another one.
And consequently the abuse continues.
We think that that kind of institutional acceptance unwillingness to hold members accountable for their actions is declining.

But, you know, again, not knowing exactly what has gone on in Mr. Foley's situation, it does appear to be the fact that some people may have known about this alleged event for a year, two years, three years, maybe even more.

And, you know, we would want to look at, well, you know, in addition to what drove the individual's behavior at the individual level - things like the cognitive distortion, sexual interest in minors, narcissism, stress - What are non-individual level, what are the community or group or peer level factors that are influencing behavior?
With juvenile delinquents, for example, we know the single best predictor of delinquent behavior is hanging out with other delinquent kids.
You know, likewise as adults, if you engage in a behavior and your peers and your superiors know about it and do nothing about it, that sends a message and it's a message of passive approval.
It sounds to me like you're intrigued by this, that this is an area where we could use more work.
I think what's interesting, yes, is that even today in 2006, we're uncovering institutions that appear to be suffering from the same kind of problem.

After all of the research, all of the treatment, all of the victim advocacy that has gone on for the past 20 years, after the Boy Scouts and churches and schools and so many groups have had to very publicly deal with their, you know, their members who offend - and yet, perhaps it has happened again, you know, here in this situation.
And it on some levels it boggles the mind that you could know about this kind of behavior and not do something to effectively end it.

I'm sure that parents who have been following this story - especially the Congressman Foley story, because the school shooting stories are so particular in their detail and so horrific it's hard to know what larger lesson to draw - but the Foley story is one that I think a lot of people would look at and really be upset by because it's so, there are situations they find themselves in.
You know you've got to give your kids more freedom.
You want them to go off on that internship.
You know, you want them to go overseas and have an adventure and so forth.
So they say to themselves, ok, you know, do I keep my kid inside the house until he's 30, you know?
Do I not let them leave my sight?
Do I not let them use the computer unless I'm sitting there?
I mean, how do you do that with a high school student?
So what do you say to people who want to know how to arm their kids against being victimized?

Those are great questions and I'm a parent of a two and a half-year-old boy and so I face - I'm looking forward to him, you know, gaining more independence, but there are things that go with that and there's greater risks.
You know, if I were the parent of a teenage page, what I would want to do is sit down with my child and ask him, you know, what have you heard in the news?
What do you know about what's going on?
Tell him what's going on and ask him did anyone ever contact you in a way that made you feel uncomfortable?
Did you ever get an e-mail or something in person was said to you or your friends that you thought was uncomfortable or weird, made you feel concerned.
I would want to convey to my child that you can always come to me.
I want you to come to me.
I'll never, you know, punish you, you know, or make fun of you if you come to me with any concerns like this.

And also to let them know that, you know, you might care about the person who does these things - much like one of the callers said earlier - you know, it may be someone that you trust, that you respect, and he's in a position of authority over you, I still want to hear about it because I'm going to make sure that you're safe.
And so the message that parents should give to their kids is come and talk to me.
When New Yorkers elect their next mayor this fall, they'll do it with machines from the 1960s.
For NPR News, I'm Brigid Bergin, in the Big Apple.
You know, check in with them regularly, are they doing ok, does anyone make them feel uncomfortable.
And then no, don't keep them at home until their 30, but let them understand that you're there for them.
And understand - and parents, of course, need to understand that it's our jobs to keep our kids safe.
We can't expect our kids to keep themselves safe.
They're easy to manipulate.
They're easy to trick.
We have to - we really have to keep an eye out.
And that means doing things like, you know, looking at the sites that your kid is going to on the Web and talk to him about that.
Professor, I'm sorry, I need to pause here to say you're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
I'd like to see if we have time for one more caller from South Bend, Indiana, and Ken.
Ken, if you'd please tell us what's on your mind and I'm afraid I do need to ask you to be brief.
Yeah.
Yes.
I was married to a woman for 14 years who was sexually abused by two brothers in law at two separate findings.
She disclosed it to her mother, who told her never to bring it up again.
And when we were married I was unaware of this.
About 14 years into our, we marriage began having problems and it only came up during our marriage counseling and was absolutely responsible for the dissolution of our marriage.
I was wondering if she might be able to comment on that.
I'll take my answer off the air.
